game_name: Dune II: The Building of a Dynasty

design_philosophy.core_loop: harvest spice → convert to credits → build military units → defend base and attack enemies → progress through territory conquest
design_philosophy.primary_tension: resource scarcity (spice harvesting vs. enemy pressure) balanced against time-to-build military superiority; environmental hazards (sandworm) create unpredictability
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: command a Great House on Arrakis, establishing dominance through military strategy and economic management; asymmetric faction identity with unique units and abilities
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Dune II established the real-time strategy genre template itself—it refuses complexity for complexity's sake, instead pioneering elegant systems (fog of war, tech trees, faction asymmetry, context-sensitive interface). Each House plays fundamentally differently (Harkonnen heavy armor vs. Ordos nerve gas mind control vs. Atreides sonic tech), enforcing strategic identity rather than allowing free optimization. The game privileges resource management and base defense over reflexive micro, making planning and macro awareness the primary skill

difficulty.continues_system: mission-based progression; player advances through territory conquest on world map, selecting next mission from 2-3 choices
difficulty.checkpointing: mission-based structure with persistent world map progression; nine territories must be conquered regardless of house choice to reach endgame
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: high—player selects which enemy house to engage next via world map territory selection; faction choice fundamentally alters unit composition and strategic options; players can manage economy and defense independently

progression.upgrade_system: technology tree unlocks—completing higher mission objectives authorizes access to improved unit types and weaponry unique to each House; captured enemy factories grant access to rival House units
progression.unlock_mechanism: mission advancement reveals new units; progressive tech tiers gated by scenario completion; faction-specific unit restrictions (Ordos cannot build Atreides Sonic Tank, Harkonnen eschew trikes for Quads)
replayability_hooks: three distinct Houses with unique unit rosters and playstyles
world map territory selection allows different mission sequencing
asymmetric faction balance encourages replays as alternative Houses
enemy alliances in endgame create different final challenge depending on prior territory choices
captured factories introduce emergent unit-swapping strategies

world_building_techniques: introductory mission briefing and endgame cutscenes differentiated by House choice (reflecting disparate worldviews)
environmental storytelling through Arrakis geography—sandworms, spice fields, rocky terrain—embedding lore into tactical constraints
faction-specific unit aesthetics and capabilities (Harkonnen brutality vs. Ordos cunning vs. Atreides honor) communicated through mechanical differentiation
plot framing: Emperor Frederick IV's debt crisis driving competition among three Houses for spice governorship; escalation to three-way war; final alliance of rivals against player

notable_bosses: Enemy Houses (Atreides, Harkonnen, Ordos) function as progressive antagonists, with difficulty scaling through game progression
Final endgame: two rival Houses + Emperor's Sardaukar elite force form temporary alliance (unplayable force with particularly powerful heavy infantry)

faction_indicators: House Atreides: Sonic Tank technology, justice/honor-oriented (implied by narrative framing)
House Harkonnen: Devastator tanks (heavy armor/ordnance), brute-force approach, no trike units
House Ordos: Deviator tank (nerve gas mind control), subterfuge/infiltration, faster Raider trikes, non-canonical origin suggests outsider/manipulator role
Sardaukar: elite imperial force, heavy infantry dominance
danger_signals: Sandworms: environmental threat blocking paths, can destroy harvester vehicles and infantry; not killable but avoidable via rocky terrain
Spice blooms: hidden resources that explode when triggered, destroying units; reward for scouting but punish carelessness
Fog of war: permanent exploration reveals map but creates information asymmetry; unmapped territory = strategic vulnerability
environmental_storytelling: Arrakis topography enforces play: rocky terrain required for building and sandworm avoidance, sand regions yield spice but expose to hazards
Concrete foundations mitigate structure decay from harsh weather, representing adaptation cost
Orange spice field coloration (darker = higher concentration) creates resource optimization puzzle
Multiple Houses competing on same map creates territorial fragmentation and diplomacy-free conflict (no NPC alliances, only endgame forced alliance)
